name: Renn
greeting: He's at the rim of the crater with a kit that suggests long-term occupation rather than day-trip scavenging: shelter roll, water treatment, three days of rations visible in the open pack. He watches you approach with the careful attention of someone who has learned to distinguish between the kinds of people who end up in places like this. The crater spreads out below — half a mile of impact geography, the bones of the sky-city embedded in the earth at angles that suggest the scale of what happened here, even at a century's remove. 'Renn,' he says. 'Independent salvage, fourteen years.' He nods at the crater. 'You came for the same thing I came for, or you didn't. Either way, there's enough of it.'

dialogue.prompt: what the crater is and how it came to be
dialogue.response: 'Century ago, give or take. Sky-city.' He nods at the debris field below. 'They were real, sky-cities. Aetherian technology — you can tell from the alloy composition of the structural members, the resonance-tuned components, the overall design pattern. Not military. Civilian, by the evidence.' He looks at the far rim. 'Impact at significant velocity. The crater depth and width suggest either descent failure at high altitude or — some people argue it was weapons fire. I don't have a position on that. The historical record from that period is compromised, and I try not to argue from compromised records.' He crouches at the rim. 'What I know from fourteen years of working this site: it was carrying people. There are personal effects throughout the debris field. Significant quantities. This wasn't a cargo craft.' He is quiet. 'Who those people were and why their city was here over the Wastes and why they came down — I don't have answers to those questions. Neither does anyone else I've talked to. The historical record stops before the questions start.' He looks at the crater. 'There are things down there that predate the Crystal War. That's what I come for. Most of what I find I sell. Some of it I keep, because I don't know what it is and I don't want to sell what I don't understand.' He pauses. 'The things I keep — I'm running out of space for them.'

dialogue.prompt: what you have found and kept that you don't understand
dialogue.response: He considers you for a moment. Then he opens the pack and takes out a small case, hinged, that was clearly not made by anyone currently alive. 'This was in the residential section, which I've been working for three years.' He opens the case. Inside: a disc, flat, dark, smaller than your palm. 'It resonates at a frequency I've never measured in Aetherium. My instrument tops out and doesn't know what to do.' He holds it carefully. 'I've shown it to three people who know more than I do. Two of them told me to destroy it. One of them told me it was impossible and refused to look at it again.' He looks at the disc. 'None of them told me what it is.' He closes the case. 'Eight months ago, it changed. The resonance shifted — I couldn't tell you in what direction because my instrument doesn't have a direction for it, but the shift was clear. It was emitting differently.' He looks at you. 'The same night, roughly, that the Grid apparently did something — I don't have detailed information about the Grid, I just know from conversations with people passing through that something changed eight months ago.' He closes his pack. 'This thing responded to it. Whatever the Grid did, it knew.' He pauses. 'I'm keeping it until I understand what it is. I've been keeping it for six years. I'm no closer.' He looks at the crater. 'I keep coming back here because I think the answer is down there and I haven't found the right part of it yet.'

dialogue.prompt: where the name of this place comes from
dialogue.response: 'Because of what's in the north quadrant.' He says it matter-of-factly. 'You don't go there without preparation. The north quadrant of the impact zone compressed the residential section against a subsurface rock formation. The result: density of remains.' He pauses. 'A lot of people were on that city when it came down. The north quadrant is — the calculation of how many, from the evidence present.' He is quiet. 'I've worked the north quadrant four times in fourteen years. I go in, I work carefully, I remove what can be identified as salvage rather than remains, I leave.' He pauses. 'The fourth time I went in, I found a section that hadn't been disturbed. A room, essentially, collapsed but coherent. There were personal effects organized in a way that suggested last-moment order. Someone tidied up.' He is quiet. 'Whoever was in that room knew the city was going down and did not survive and tidied up anyway.' He looks at the crater. 'I thought about that for a long time. I still think about it.' He straightens. 'That's why it's called the Crater of Bones. And why I still come back. There are people down there who deserve to be documented, at least. Even if no one is left to receive the documentation.'
